parents.father_research_notes: Daniel Brown of Sandisfield MA (one of earliest settlers).

notable.notable_blurb: First to charge Benedict Arnold with misconduct

career.notes: Moved to Pittsfield 1773; member of Massachusetts General Court; county judge 1779.
Rose to Colonel. Key roles at Fort Ticonderoga (1775), Canadian campaign (1775-76), Saratoga campaign (1777), Stone Arabia (1780).

education.institution_blurb: Yale College class of 1771; classmate of David Humphreys.

military_service.notes: Participated in capture of Fort Ticonderoga May 10, 1775. Selected by Ethan Allen to carry Ticonderoga news to Continental Congress. Canadian campaign 1775-76; Battle of Chambly Oct 19, 1775 (key to fall of Fort St. John). Saratoga campaign 1777 — raid on Ticonderoga and nearby outposts. Killed at Battle of Stone Arabia, Mohawk Valley, October 19, 1780 (his birthday). First to formally bring charges against Benedict Arnold.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in battle on his own birthday, 1780
narrative_blocks.body: John Brown of Pittsfield was killed in action on October 19, 1780 — the same day as his thirty-sixth birthday — at a battle in the Mohawk Valley of New York, ambushed with forty-five of his men while defending the region against Loyalist raids. He was born and died on the same date, thirty-six years apart.

narrative_blocks.header: He was Benedict Arnold's enemy before Arnold was
narrative_blocks.body: John Brown was the first American to formally bring charges against Benedict Arnold — accusing him of misconduct during the 1775 Canadian campaign, years before Arnold's famous defection to the British. Their feud ran through the entire war; Brown eventually resigned his commission rather than continue serving in Arnold's shadow.

narrative_blocks.header: First to see Fort Ticonderoga was vulnerable
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1775, Brown was the first to report in writing that Fort Ticonderoga on Lake Champlain could and should be seized — writing in a secret dispatch that it 'must be taken as soon as possible.' When the fort fell to Ethan Allen and the Green Mountain Boys on May 10, 1775, Allen chose Brown to carry the victory news to the Continental Congress in Philadelphia.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter grew up fatherless; married Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Brown was killed when his daughter Sarah was barely a year old — she never knew her father. Sarah Brown grew up in Pittsfield and married Thomas Hooker of Northampton, Massachusetts, in 1796, connecting the Brown and Hooker families to the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker of Hartford.

burial.plot_notes: Killed at Stone Arabia NY, October 19, 1780. Burial location not documented.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
